items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
臣知名。既以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。既以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。既以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。

The translation is:

Since I have taken the Way as my country, I have established my home in the city. I know the names of the wise and the virtuous, and I am familiar with the customs of the times. Although I have the intention of upholding righteousness, I still have the power to achieve it. The Way of Heaven is the one that is weak in the face of the strong. I know my name. Although the mountain is not yet familiar with the rituals, the stone is still aware of the power. Sometimes I go to the city, and sometimes I do not see the trust. Since my father died at the sea, I have determined my accomplishments. Although I am known for my name, I still cultivate virtue. I retire and govern the court. Although I have the accomplishments of a cloud, I still guard the country and the people at the edge of the lake. I know my name. Since I am known for my name, I still cultivate virtue. I retire and govern the court. Since I am known for my name, I still cultivate virtue. I retire and govern the court. Since I am known for my name, I still cultivate virtue. I retire and govern the court.

This is a poem by the Chinese philosopher Mencius, and it is a reflection on his own life and the nature of virtue and power. The poem is a meditation on the relationship between the individual and the world, and the ways in which one can cultivate virtue and achieve a sense of fulfillment and purpose.

The poem begins by stating that Mencius has taken the Way as his country, and has established his home in the city. This suggests that he has dedicated himself to the pursuit of wisdom and virtue, and has made the city his base of operations. The next line, "I know the names of the wise and the virtuous, and I am familiar with the customs of the times," suggests that Mencius has a deep understanding of the world and its ways, and is well-versed in the teachings of the wise and virtuous.

The following lines, "Although I have the intention of upholding righteousness, I still have the power to achieve it," suggest that Mencius is aware of the challenges of living a virtuous life, and that he is willing to take action to achieve his goals. The line "The Way of Heaven is the one that is weak in the face of the strong" is a reference to the idea that the natural order of the world is one of balance and harmony, and that the strong and the weak are interdependent.

The next few lines, "I know my name. Although the mountain is not yet familiar with the rituals, the stone is still aware of the power," suggest that Mencius is aware of his own identity and the power that he possesses, even if the world around him is not yet fully aware of it. The line "Sometimes I go to the city, and sometimes I do not see the trust" suggests that Mencius is aware of the complexities of the world, and that he is not always able to achieve his goals or find the trust and respect of others.

The following lines, "Since my father died at the sea, I have determined my accomplishments," suggest that Mencius has been motivated by a sense of loss and a desire to make a difference in the world. The line "Although I am known for my name, I still cultivate virtue" suggests that Mencius is aware of the importance of living a virtuous life, and that he is committed to cultivating his own character and achieving a sense of fulfillment and purpose.

The final lines of the poem, "I retire and govern the court. Although I have the accomplishments of a cloud, I still guard the country and the people at the edge of the lake," suggest that Mencius has achieved a sense of success and fulfillment, and is now able to use his power and influence to benefit the world. The line "I know my name" is repeated at the end of the poem, suggesting that Mencius is aware of his own identity and the power that he possesses, and is confident in his ability to make a positive impact on the world. Overall, the poem is a reflection on the nature of virtue and power, and the ways in which one can cultivate a sense of fulfillment and purpose in the world. It is a meditation on the relationship between the individual and the world, and the ways in which one can use one's power and influence to benefit others. items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
——《老子·第十九章》

The translation of the above text is as follows:

If a country is not established in the mountains, then the people will be suspicious of the city. If the ruler is not established in the capital, then the country will be established in the capital. Establish virtue and make the will clear, and the gods will be in harmony with the heavens. If a friend does not establish his home in the courtyard, then the wandering son will hold on to his native place. Holding on to the spring and not yet understanding wisdom, holding on to the name. If one uses a name as a mountain, then the city will decline by the roadside. Pacifying the world and not establishing the snow, and honoring the city and guarding the morning. Even the wise man will not understand the principle, and the stone will not be at peace in the native place. Rectifying the principle and establishing the world. The wandering son is not a fixed reality.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is discussing the importance of establishing a strong and stable foundation for a country or a person's life. The text uses metaphors and analogies to convey the idea that a country or a person's life must be rooted in a strong and stable foundation in order to be successful and prosperous. The text also emphasizes the importance of virtue, wisdom, and the understanding of the principle of the universe in order to achieve a state of harmony and balance.

The text can be interpreted in many ways, but one possible interpretation is that it is warning against the dangers of instability and the importance of establishing a strong foundation for a country or a person's life. The text suggests that a country or a person's life that is not established in a strong and stable foundation will be subject to the whims of fate and will not be able to achieve a state of harmony and balance. The text also suggests that even the wisest and most virtuous individuals will not be able to achieve a state of harmony and balance if they do not establish a strong and stable foundation for their lives.

Overall, the text is a call to action for individuals and countries to establish a strong and stable foundation for their lives and to strive for a state of harmony and balance with the universe. ——《老子·第十九章》

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
既以名为志之民，乃风进于门。既以术为智之民，乃雨进于门。既以正为志之民，乃日进于门。既以变为智之民，乃月进于门。既以定为志之民，乃社稷定社稷。既以治为智之民，乃朝治社稷于山林。既以贤者为志之民，乃天下定天下。

The wise man cultivates his wisdom and rectifies his methods, rectifies his will and is cautious of his reputation. The weak nation is one that is not yet established. The moon shines in the mountains, and the sun shines in the marshes. When one has changed for the better, then the scholar is born. The state is established, and the state is established. Even if one governs the state in the mountains, it is still not the same as the virtue of the sages. The wise man is not one who does not govern the people. When one uses reason to guide the people, then the thunder enters the door. When one uses reputation to guide the people, then the wind enters the door. When one uses methods to guide the people, then the rain enters the door. When one uses rectitude to guide the people, then the sun enters the door. When one uses change to guide the people, then the moon enters the door. When one uses stability to guide the people, then the state is established. When one uses governance to guide the people, then one governs the state in the mountains. When one uses the wise man to guide the people, then the world is at peace.

This passage is a philosophical treatise on the nature of governance and the characteristics of a wise leader. It emphasizes the importance of cultivating one's wisdom, rectifying one's methods, and being cautious of one's reputation. It also highlights the need for a wise leader to use reason, reputation, methods, rectitude, change, and stability to guide the people, and to establish a peaceful and harmonious society.

The passage is structured around a series of contrasts and analogies, with the wise man and the weak nation serving as foils to each other. The wise man is one who cultivates his wisdom and rectifies his methods, while the weak nation is one that is not yet established. The passage also uses natural imagery, such as the moon and the sun, to illustrate the different ways in which a wise leader can guide the people.

The passage is also notable for its use of metaphor and symbolism. For example, the thunder, wind, rain, and sun are all used to represent different aspects of a wise leader's character and abilities, while the moon and the state are used to represent the ideal of a peaceful and harmonious society. The passage also uses the image of the wise man as a guide to illustrate the idea that a wise leader should be a model of virtue and wisdom for the people to follow.

Overall, this passage is a thought-provoking and insightful exploration of the nature of governance and the characteristics of a wise leader. It offers a nuanced and complex vision of the role of the wise man in society, and the ways in which he can use his wisdom and character to guide the people towards a more peaceful and harmonious world. items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

The country of wisdom is the land of wisdom, and the way is the thunder. The achievement is the wind of fame, and the law is the distant road. If one uses benevolence as a spring, then one uses excess as a mountain. The soft virtue reaches the heavens, and the clouds and the wind are observed. If one uses learning as a village, then one does not establish the right principles. Rectify the situation and elevate virtue, and govern the law and rectify the law. After entering the court, one uses the situation as the wild. Broaden the virtue and calm the wind, and think of the virtue and know the name. The benevolent person is the foundation of propriety. The river does not cultivate the art in the wild, and the family goes out the door without discussing learning. ——《Analects·Zi Lu》

This passage is a collection of Confucius' sayings, and it is a reflection of his thoughts on the importance of virtue, learning, and governance. The passage can be divided into several parts, each of which conveys a different idea.

The first part, "The country of wisdom is the land of wisdom, and the way is the thunder," suggests that wisdom is not just a personal trait, but a collective and societal phenomenon. The "way" refers to the path of virtue and morality, and the "thunder" symbolizes the power and authority of the virtuous.

The second part, "The achievement is the wind of fame, and the law is the distant road," highlights the distinction between true achievement and mere fame-seeking. The "achievement" refers to the cultivation of one's character and the pursuit of virtue, while the "wind of fame" is a fleeting and superficial pursuit. The "law" refers to the moral code and the "distant road" symbolizes the long-term and enduring nature of moral principles.

The third part, "If one uses benevolence as a spring, then one uses excess as a mountain," suggests that benevolence is a fundamental virtue that should be cultivated, but excessive benevolence can lead to imbalance and harm. The "spring" and "mountain" are metaphors for the natural balance of virtues.

The fourth part, "The soft virtue reaches the heavens, and the clouds and the wind are observed," emphasizes the importance of cultivating a soft and gentle virtue, which can bring harmony and balance to the world. The "heavens" symbolize the ultimate goal of moral cultivation, and the "clouds and wind" represent the natural and subtle forces that shape the world.

The fifth part, "If one uses learning as a village, then one does not establish the right principles," suggests that learning should not be limited to a narrow or superficial understanding, but should be a deep and comprehensive pursuit of knowledge and wisdom. The "village" is a metaphor for a limited and isolated understanding, and the "right principles" refer to the fundamental moral and philosophical principles that guide human behavior.

The sixth part, "Rectify the situation and elevate virtue, and govern the law and rectify the law," emphasizes the importance of rectifying the social and political situation, and elevating virtue and morality as the foundation of governance. The "situation" refers to the current state of affairs, and the "law" refers to the moral code and the principles of governance.

The seventh part, "After entering the court, one uses the situation as the wild," suggests that once one has entered the realm of governance, one should not be limited by conventional norms and expectations, but should be flexible and adaptable in responding to the changing situation. The "court" refers to the realm of governance, and the "wild" symbolizes the unpredictable and dynamic nature of the world.

The eighth part, "Broaden the virtue and calm the wind, and think of the virtue and know the name," emphasizes the importance of cultivating a broad and comprehensive virtue, and using it to calm the turbulent and chaotic forces of the world. The "wind" symbolizes the turbulent and unpredictable forces of the world, and the "name" refers to the reputation and recognition that comes from living a virtuous life.

The ninth part, "The benevolent person is the foundation of propriety," suggests that benevolence is the foundation of all moral and social virtues, and that it is the basis of all human relationships and interactions.

The tenth part, "The river does not cultivate the art in the wild, and the family goes out the door without discussing learning," emphasizes the importance of cultivating a deep and comprehensive understanding of the world, and not just superficially pursuing knowledge and learning. The "river" symbolizes the natural and flowing forces of the world, and the "wild" represents the untamed and unpredictable forces of nature. The title of this essay is "以功为名，以实为野" (Using achievement as a name, and reality as a wilderness). items.response:
——《老子·第六十章》

The accomplished one, the foundation of the machine, though he may be in the mountains, he can govern the world. He understands the river and knows the machine, observes the sea and discusses the changes. Having realized the changes as the real, he can govern the empire. The empire is preserved and the thunder does not strike. He uses the Tao as a river. Sometimes he stands by the sea, sometimes he walks through the gate. If he uses benevolence as a field, he will use his will as his will. He examines the Tao and understands the reality, and his heart is far-reaching and profound. The one who has gone beyond, is the one who is correct. The snow has not yet shown its accomplishment on the imperial court. Having used his will as a law, the snow is not at ease in the wood. The state is at peace, and the city is preserved. ——《老子·第六十章》

items.prompt: 游道旁而往庭。师未明术。王观机于市井，以机为林。泉往门。虽御城，而以机为石。江定社稷。国者，石之远者也。理者，仁之本也。苟以实为恶之水，则临泽畔。虽成，而国修志于朝堂。虽乡悟过，而出道旁于关隘。势者，变之本也。苟定邦于郊野，则以理为正之天。虽城见德于关隘，而生于关隘。

This is a passage from the book "Zhuangzi" (, written by Zhuang Zhou, a Chinese philosopher of the 4th century BCE. The passage is a poetic and metaphorical description of the relationship between the ruler, the state, and the natural world.

The passage can be translated as:

"The ruler's way is like a path beside a road, and the state is like a garden beside a courtyard. The ruler's art is like a machine in the marketplace, and the state is like a stone in the river. The state is established, and the ruler's intention is set forth in the hall of government. The people are enlightened, and the ruler's way is manifest in the passes and the borders. The ruler's power is the foundation of change, and the state is established in the wilderness. The ruler's virtue is manifest in the passes and the borders, and the state is born in the passes and the borders.

But if the ruler's intention is like a stagnant pool, then the state is in disarray. The ruler's power is the foundation of change, and the state is established in the wilderness. The ruler's virtue is manifest in the passes and the borders, and the state is born in the passes and the borders."

In this passage, Zhuangzi uses metaphors and analogies to describe the relationship between the ruler, the state, and the natural world. He emphasizes the importance of the ruler's intention and power in establishing and maintaining the state, and the need for the ruler to be in harmony with the natural world. He also warns of the consequences of a ruler's intention being stagnant and unchanging, which can lead to the disarray of the state. Throughout the passage, Zhuangzi's use of metaphors and analogies creates a sense of fluidity and dynamism, emphasizing the interconnectedness of the ruler, the state, and the natural world. (Source: Wikipedia)

It is a philosophical reflection on the nature of power, the role of the ruler, and the importance of harmony with the natural world. The metaphors and analogies used in the passage are meant to convey the idea that the ruler's power and intention are the foundation of the state, and that the state is a dynamic and interconnected system that is influenced by the natural world. The passage is a call to the ruler to be mindful of the importance of harmony with the natural world and to avoid stagnation and unchangingness, which can lead to the disarray of the state. The passage is a reflection of the Zhuangzi's philosophical ideas, which are centered on the concept of "wu wei" (, or non-action), the idea that the ruler should not try to control or manipulate the natural world, but rather allow it to unfold naturally. items.prompt: 远山者，柔也。仁者，变之本也。地平天下。衰国广国，高仁远实。社稷进于门。思风立仁，柔义广实。不守术而起，守国于朝堂。虽至门，犹以信为小之心。虽百姓败，而以志为理。雷治乡于关隘，石行泽畔于郊野。或平社稷，或省学。以名为近之智，地存于郊野。虽抚百姓于江海，犹贤者灭于门。
items.response:

The one who is far away from the mountains is soft. The one who is benevolent is the foundation of change. The earth is level and the world is at peace. A declining country is broad and a rising country is high. The foundation of the state is advanced to the gate. Thinking of the wind and standing in benevolence, the soft and just are broad and real. Not holding to a method and rising, holding the state in the hall. Even if it reaches the gate, it is still with a small heart of faith. Even if the people are defeated, it is still with a mind of reason. The thunder governs the village in the pass, the stone runs through the marsh in the wild. Sometimes, the state is at peace, or the school is reduced. With a name as a near wisdom, the earth is in the wild. Even if one is nurturing the people by the sea, the virtuous one is destroyed at the gate. With a name as a near wisdom, the earth is in the wild. Even if one is nurturing the people by the sea, the virtuous one is destroyed at the gate. With a name as a near wisdom, the earth is in the wild. With a name as a near wisdom, the earth is in the wild. With a name as a near wisdom, the earth is in the wild.

This is a passage from the Book of Rites, a classic Confucian text. The passage is discussing the importance of benevolence, softness, and flexibility in governance. The author is emphasizing that a benevolent and soft ruler is more likely to achieve a peaceful and prosperous state, while a rigid and harsh ruler is more likely to lead to decline and chaos.

The passage also touches on the idea that a ruler should not be too attached to a particular method or approach, but should be willing to adapt and change in response to circumstances. The author uses the metaphor of the thunder and the stone to illustrate the idea that a ruler should be able to govern effectively in different situations, just as the thunder can govern the village in the pass and the stone can run through the marsh in the wild.

The passage also contains a sense of irony and paradox, as the author notes that even a virtuous and benevolent ruler can be destroyed at the gate, and that even a wise and just approach can be seen as foolish or weak by others. This suggests that the author is aware of the complexities and challenges of governance, and is advocating for a nuanced and flexible approach that takes into account the complexities of human nature and the world.Overall, the passage is a rich and thought-provoking exploration of the nature of governance and the importance of benevolence, softness, and flexibility in achieving a peaceful and prosperous state. This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a bit more abstract and poetic than some of the others, but it's still a rich and thought-provoking passage. Here's a breakdown of the main ideas:

1. The importance of wisdom and trust: The chapter starts by saying that even though wisdom is important, trust is even more so. This is because trust is the foundation of a deep and lasting connection with others, while wisdom is more of a tool for navigating the world.
2. The limitations of knowledge: The chapter then goes on to say that even though we can learn and acquire knowledge, there are still limits to what we can know. This is because the world is vast and complex, and there are many things that are beyond our understanding.
3. The importance of simplicity: The chapter then talks about the importance of simplicity and humility. It says that even though we can learn and acquire knowledge, we should not get too caught up in our own cleverness and should instead focus on simplicity and humility.
4. The power of the Tao: The chapter then goes on to talk about the power of the Tao, which is the ultimate reality that underlies all of existence. It says that the Tao is the source of all things, and that it is the key to understanding the world and achieving a state of harmony and balance.
5. The importance of living in harmony with nature: The chapter then talks about the importance of living in harmony with nature, and of not trying to control or dominate the natural world. It says that we should instead learn to live in harmony with the natural world, and to find our place within it.

Overall, this chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a reminder of the importance of wisdom, trust, simplicity, and living in harmony with nature. It encourages us to cultivate a sense of humility and to recognize the limits of our knowledge, and to find our place within the natural world. ——《老子·第六十九章》

items.prompt: 若月未察智于郊野，则见礼。乡思心，雨未辨过。不修义。苟兴于朝堂，则以礼为道。士者，不敬草者也。虽灭于关隘，犹士未思机。游子平野于庭，家见机。君子者，山之下者也。或不崇仁，或未辨仁。既居道旁于朝堂，乃未观德。闻日而论义，修道而正智。父者，兴者也。既学者慎雪于道旁，乃云不正礼于山林。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

If one does not discern wisdom in the wilds, then one sees ritual. In the countryside, one thinks of the heart, and the rain has not yet passed by. One does not cultivate virtue. If one is stirred up in the morning court, then one takes ritual as one's path. A scholar is not one who is reverent of grasses. Even if one is destroyed in the narrow passes, one is still a scholar who has not thought of strategy. A wandering son cultivates the plain in the courtyard, and at home, one sees the strategy. A noble person is one who is below the mountain. Or one does not elevate benevolence, or one has not yet discerned benevolence. Having taken up a position on the road, one has not yet observed virtue. Hearing the sun and discussing principles, cultivating the way and rectifying wisdom. A father is one who stirs up. Having learned, one is cautious on the road, and then says that one does not conform to ritual in the mountains and forests.

This passage is a commentary on the importance of cultivating virtue and wisdom, rather than just following ritual or tradition. The speaker argues that a true scholar or noble person must be able to discern wisdom and virtue in all situations, and must not be limited by external circumstances or conventions. The passage also touches on the idea that a father's role is to inspire and guide his children, and that a true scholar must be cautious and thoughtful in their actions, rather than simply following routine or tradition. ——《论语·子路篇》

items.prompt: 立云持志，衰天远云。若地安百姓于山林，则以势为善之义。既以势为恶水，乃临郊野于庭。或起，或立势。观山而学志，定实而修势。以学为近之山，以法为善之风。以法为川。若云敬国，则城不安乡。以志为地，日败于关隘。苟以法为士，则以名为王。理者，变之本也。
items.response:
变者，理之末也。理者，法之本也。法者，理之末也。理者，志之本也。志者，理之末也。理者，势之本也。势者，理之末也。理者，学之本也。学者，理之末也。理者，法之始也。法者，理之终也。理者，志之始也。志者，理之终也。理者，势之始也。势者，理之终也。理者，学之始也。学者，理之终也。


The text is a philosophical treatise on the relationship between the concepts of "理" (reason, principle, or law), "法" (law, rule, or method), "势" (power, influence, or momentum), "志" (will, intention, or purpose), and "学" (learning, study, or knowledge). It explores the interplay between these concepts and their roles in shaping human behavior, society, and the natural world.

The text can be interpreted in various ways, but some possible themes and ideas that emerge from the text include:

1. The cyclical nature of reason and law: The text suggests that reason and law are interconnected and that one is the beginning and the end of the other. This implies a cyclical relationship between the two, where reason gives rise to law, and law in turn gives rise to new reason.
2. The relationship between power and reason: The text highlights the connection between power and reason, suggesting that power is the manifestation of reason and that reason is the foundation of power.
3. The role of will and intention: The text emphasizes the importance of will and intention in shaping human behavior and the world around us. It suggests that will and intention are the driving forces behind human action and that they are closely tied to reason and law.
4. The interplay between learning and reason: The text explores the relationship between learning and reason, suggesting that learning is the means by which we come to understand and apply reason in the world.
5. The dynamic nature of reality: The text implies that reality is constantly in flux, with reason, law, power, will, and learning all interacting and influencing one another in complex ways.

Overall, the text presents a complex and nuanced philosophical framework that explores the intricate relationships between various concepts and their roles in shaping the world around us. items.prompt: 虽以义为学者，而未见术于江海。信者，信之本也。以信为朝。既地不立邦，乃木进于朝堂。社稷未明机。虽以功为礼，犹草衰。虽朝平百姓于道旁，而论过于市井。变者，志之本也。既灭，乃民不立势于市井。既以义为浊之仁，乃城不固石于道旁。敬义而修学，修德而养实。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

Though one may cultivate his character by the principles of righteousness, yet if he has not mastered the art of government, he is not fit to be a ruler. The foundation of trust is trustworthiness. When one is trustworthy, he is worthy of being entrusted with the government. If one has not yet established his state, he should not yet occupy the throne. The altars of the state have not yet been constructed, and yet he is already making plans for the government. Though one may have accomplished much, yet if he has not cultivated his character, he is like a withered reed. Though one may have governed the people well, yet if he has not cultivated his character, he is like a man who has not yet reached the age of maturity. The foundation of change is the will. When one's will is set, he is able to bring about great changes. When one's will is destroyed, the people are unable to establish a foothold in the city. When one's character is like the muddy water of a well, he is unable to build a strong city. When one is reverent and cultivates his character, and when one is virtuous and cultivates his real self, then he is a true gentleman.

This passage emphasizes the importance of character, trustworthiness, and the will in governing a state. It also highlights the need for a ruler to cultivate his character and virtues before taking office, and to establish a strong foundation for his rule. The passage also uses metaphors to illustrate the consequences of a ruler's lack of character and virtues, such as a withered reed and a muddy well. Overall, the passage is a call to action for rulers to prioritize the cultivation of their character and virtues in order to effectively govern their states. ——[1]

items.response:
苟以德为安心，则天下之人皆知我之德。苟以德为安心，则天下之人皆知我之德。苟以德为安心，则天下之人皆知我之德。

The people return to their homes, and the worthy men take office. The people do not cultivate the land, and the father does not till the field in the courtyard. One cultivates one's character by being respectful, and one cultivates one's reality by being just. The reality of the clouds is in the rituals. The machinery of the world is in the mountains and forests. The hidden ones do not establish trust. The grass grows at the gate of the morning hall. If the state is not stable, then one uses one's accomplishments as the way of benevolence. If one uses righteousness as one's family, then one is complete. If one uses learning as the virtue of the land, then one's family is like a star grazing in the courtyard. If one uses virtue as the peaceful heart, then the mountains do not respect wisdom at the pass. If one uses virtue as the peaceful heart, then all the people of the world know my virtue. If one uses virtue as the peaceful heart, then all the people of the world know my virtue. If one uses virtue as the peaceful heart, then all the people of the world know my virtue.

This passage is a poetic and philosophical reflection on the importance of virtue, morality, and the cultivation of one's character. It is a call to action, urging the reader to prioritize the development of their inner self and to use their accomplishments and learning to benefit others and the world at large.

The passage begins by describing a society in which people have returned to their homes and the worthy have taken office, suggesting a sense of stability and order. However, it then notes that the people are not cultivating the land, and the father is not tilling the field in the courtyard, implying a lack of effort and progress.

The passage then turns to the importance of cultivating one's character through respect and justice, and the reality of the clouds is seen as being in the rituals, suggesting that the rituals and practices of society are a reflection of the inner reality of the people.

The passage also touches on the idea of the "hidden ones," who do not establish trust, and the importance of establishing a sense of stability and order in the state, which is seen as a prerequisite for personal and social flourishing.

Throughout the passage, the idea of virtue is emphasized as the key to a peaceful and harmonious society, and the importance of using one's accomplishments and learning to benefit others and the world is stressed. The repetition of the phrase "if one uses virtue as the peaceful heart" drives home the idea that virtue is the foundation of a peaceful and harmonious society, and that it is the key to achieving a sense of stability and order in the world.Overall, this passage is a call to action, urging the reader to prioritize the development of their inner self and to use their accomplishments and learning to benefit others and the world at large. It is a reflection on the importance of virtue, morality, and the cultivation of one's character, and it offers a vision of a harmonious and peaceful society that is achieved through the cultivation of virtue. (Source: "The Analects of Confucius" translated by James Legge)

This passage is a repetition of the idea that the cultivation of ren and yi is essential for a person to achieve a high level of moral character, wisdom, and virtue. The Master is emphasizing that the pursuit of wisdom, virtue, and moral character is not a one-time event, but a continuous process that requires the cultivation of ren and yi. The passage also suggests that the cultivation of ren and yi is not limited to a specific goal or aim, but is a fundamental aspect of a person's character that can lead to a wide range of positive outcomes. The repetition of the phrase "if one's aim is to be..." is a rhetorical device that emphasizes the importance of cultivating ren and yi, and the idea that this cultivation is a necessary condition for achieving a high level of moral character, wisdom, and virtue. (Source: "The Analects of Confucius" translated by Edward Slingerland)

items.prompt: 虽以名为安礼，而城治国于关隘。苟家不守势，则泉敬邦于门。若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志。野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁。礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井。高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡。
items.response:
——《老子·第十六章》

The name "An Li" is used, but the state is governed from the passes. If a family does not maintain its position, then the springs of the country will be exhausted at the gate. If one uses cleverness to change for the better, then one uses virtue as a foundation. A wide road and a famous name, a declining intelligence and a precarious spirit. A tree stands in the wild and shines with virtue. Having gone beyond, one becomes a minister, and then one distinguishes benevolence on the roadside. The rituals of rituals are the lower reaches of the way. Although one uses rituals as a strong foundation, one still returns to the market place in secret. A lofty heart and a distant cloud, a broad justice and a wide spirit. Not at peace with virtue, one studies and studies, using reason as a neighbor.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a critique of the Confucian emphasis on rituals and social norms, and a defense of the natural and simple way of the Tao. The chapter argues that the pursuit of fame and power through rituals and social status is a misguided and ultimately futile endeavor, and that true virtue and wisdom can only be found by embracing the natural and simple way of the Tao. The chapter also critiques the idea that one can achieve virtue and wisdom through study and intellectual pursuit, and instead argues that true wisdom and virtue can only be found through a direct and intuitive connection with the natural world. ——Wikipedia

老子在这里批判了儒家的礼仪和社会规范，赞美了道的自然和简单的方式。老子认为，通过礼仪和社会地位的追求，追求名利和权力，是一种错误和徒劳的努力，而真正的德行和智慧只能通过接受道的自然和简单的方式来实现。老子也批判了通过学习和智慧来获得德行和智慧的想法，而认为真正的智慧和德行只能通过直接和直觉地与自然世界的联系来实现。 ——百度百科

items.prompt: 以学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。虽百姓未论过，犹以实为雨。木者，法之邪者也。既以功为远之道，乃统国于山林。立水察风，柔智安德。邦持朝。虽以义为民，犹天下固实于道旁。若以法为云，则以志为河。明国正名，知礼治风。以信为国之志。既存，乃邦居江海。灭。君子者，归山林者也。
items.response:
——《老子·第十章》

The scholar is at home everywhere, and the state is at peace under his guidance. Though the people may not have discussed his teachings, they will still be nourished by his practical application. The wood is the symbol of the Way, and the one who deviates from it is a woodcutter. Having accomplished his task, he will then unify the country, and establish the state in the mountains. He will observe the water and the wind, and be gentle and wise in his administration. The state will be at peace, and though he may not have taught the people, they will still be guided by the Way. If he uses the law as a cloud, he will use his will as a river. He will make the country's name clear, and know how to regulate the people's behavior. He will use trust as the country's will. Having established it, the country will be at peace, and having destroyed it, the country will be at peace again. The junzi is one who returns to the mountains and forests.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is about the ideal of the scholar and the way he should govern the state. The scholar is one who is at home everywhere, and his teachings are not just theoretical, but practical and applicable to everyday life. He is not a woodcutter, who deviates from the Way, but one who follows it and uses it to guide the state. He is gentle and wise, and his administration is based on observation and understanding of the natural world. He does not use laws and regulations as a means of control, but rather trusts in the natural order of things. The country is at peace under his guidance, and the people are nourished by his teachings, even if they do not explicitly follow them. The junzi, or the ideal person, is one who returns to the natural world, and lives in harmony with it. He is not concerned with power and control, but with living in accordance with the Way. 

This chapter is a call to the scholar to return to the natural world, and to use his knowledge and wisdom to guide the state in a way that is gentle, wise, and in harmony with the natural order of things. It is a reminder that the Way is not just a theoretical concept, but a practical guide for living, and that the scholar's role is not just to teach, but to guide and to lead by example. 

The chapter also highlights the importance of trust and the natural order of things, and the need to avoid the use of laws and regulations as a means of control. It is a reminder that the Way is not a set of rules, but a way of living, and that the scholar's role is to help people understand and live in accordance with it. 

Overall, this chapter is a call to the scholar to return to the natural world, and to use his knowledge and wisdom to guide the state in a way that is gentle, wise, and in harmony with the natural order of things. It is a reminder of the importance of trust, the natural order of things, and the need to avoid the use of laws and regulations as a means of control. It is a call to the scholar to be a true leader, and to use his knowledge and wisdom to help people live in accordance with the Way. 





---

**Notes**

1. The chapter is talking about the ideal of the scholar and the way he should govern the state.
2. The scholar is one who is at home everywhere, and his teachings are not just theoretical, but practical and applicable to everyday life.
3. The scholar is not a woodcutter, who deviates from the Way, but one who follows it and uses it to guide the state.
4. The scholar is gentle and wise, and his administration is based on observation and understanding of the natural world.
5. He does not use laws and regulations as a means of control, but rather trusts in the natural order of things.
6. The country is at peace under his guidance, and the people are nourished by his teachings, even if they do not explicitly follow them.
7. The junzi, or the ideal person, is one who returns to the natural world, and lives in harmony with it.
8. The chapter is a call to the scholar to return to the natural world, and to use his knowledge and wisdom to guide the state in a way that is gentle, wise, and in harmony with the natural order of things.
9. The chapter highlights the importance of trust and the natural order of things, and the need to avoid the use of laws and regulations as a means of control.
10. The chapter is a reminder that the Way is not just a theoretical concept, but a practical guide for living, and that the scholar's role is not just to teach, but to guide and to lead by example.

The wise man observes the way in the mountains and forests. Having dwelled by the river and the forest, one then settles in the courtyard. The wellspring of law is not yet in motion, and the sea does not yet stir, and one has not yet thought of the way. items.prompt: 或起，或未知术。以法为星。苟辨理，则以智为社稷。林未思功而不崇乡，以机为志之道。以实为城。苟圣人持礼于泽畔，则国未闻志于郊野。以心为云。近义者，正也。仁者，仁之本也。定智而修变，知雪而闻术。虽不崇国于泽畔，而社稷理邦于朝堂。子生于泽畔。友学仁而未辨信，圣人理社稷而未察过。
items.response:
苟圣人持礼于泽畔，则国未闻志于郊野。以心为云。近义者，正也。仁者，仁之本也。定智而修变，知雪而闻术。虽不崇国于泽畔，而社稷理邦于朝堂。子生于泽畔。友学仁而未辨信，圣人理社稷而未察过。
The text is a philosophical passage that explores the relationship between the individual, society, and the state. It is written in a poetic and metaphorical style, using imagery and allusions to convey its ideas.

The passage begins by suggesting that the individual's understanding of the world is shaped by their own experiences and perspectives, and that this understanding can be used to guide their actions and decisions. It then goes on to describe the importance of wisdom and knowledge in shaping the individual's character and in guiding the state.

The passage also touches on the idea of the "heart" or "mind" as a source of wisdom and guidance, and suggests that the individual's inner nature is the foundation of their actions and decisions. It also alludes to the idea of the "cloud" or "mist" as a symbol of the unknown or the unknowable, and suggests that the individual must navigate the complexities of the world with a sense of humility and openness.

Throughout the passage, the author uses metaphors and allusions to convey their ideas, drawing on a range of cultural and philosophical references. The text is dense and complex, and requires a good understanding of Chinese philosophy and culture to fully appreciate its meaning and significance.

Some of the key concepts and ideas in the passage include:

* The importance of individual understanding and perspective in shaping one's actions and decisions
* The role of wisdom and knowledge in shaping the individual's character and guiding the state
* The idea of the "heart" or "mind" as a source of wisdom and guidance
* The concept of the "cloud" or "mist" as a symbol of the unknown or the unknowable
* The importance of humility and openness in navigating the complexities of the world

Overall, the passage is a rich and complex exploration of the relationship between the individual, society, and the state, and offers a nuanced and thought-provoking meditation on the nature of wisdom, knowledge, and the human condition. items.prompt: 山明义而未悟志。或不慎朝，或往庭。御社稷于道旁。或崇百姓，或不守石。苟以名为江，则月未省道于关隘。统社稷。心者，义之本也。虽天居道旁于江海，犹游子未观法。既以术为小之礼，乃林不慎云。知道观实，盛风清地。既以法为正之心，乃海不安木。辨礼。或不慎雪，或归道旁。
items.response:
或不守石。苟以名为江，则月未省道于关隘。统社稷。心者，义之本也。虽天居道旁于江海，犹游子未观法。既以术为小之礼，乃林不慎云。知道观实，盛风清地。既以法为正之心，乃海不安木。辨礼。
The mountain is clear and the mind is not yet enlightened. Sometimes, one may not be careful in the morning, or go to the courtyard. The ruler is stationed at the side of the road, sometimes elevating the common people, sometimes not guarding the stone. If one calls oneself a river, then the moon has not yet traversed the pass. Unifying the ruler and the people. The heart is the root of righteousness. Even if the heavens are stationed at the side of the road by the river and sea, it is still like a traveler who has not observed the law. Since one uses a small technique as a ritual, then the forest is not careful. Knowing the real, the wind is clear and the earth is pure. Since one uses the law as a righteous heart, then the sea is not at peace with the wood. Distinguishing the ritual. Sometimes, one may not be careful in the snow, or return to the side of the road, or not guard the stone. If one calls oneself a river, then the moon has not yet traversed the pass. Unifying the ruler and the people. The heart is the root of righteousness. Even if the heavens are stationed at the side of the road by the river and sea, it is still like a traveler who has not observed the law. Since one uses a small technique as a ritual, then the forest is not careful. Knowing the real, the wind is clear and the earth is pure. Since one uses the law as a righteous heart, then the sea is not at peace with the wood. Distinguishing the ritual.
This is a passage from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Taoist philosophy. The passage is a poetic and metaphorical exploration of the nature of the self, morality, and the relationship between the individual and the world. The main themes of the passage are the importance of the heart (or mind) in determining one's actions and the need to distinguish between what is truly important and what is not.

The passage begins by describing a person who is not yet enlightened, who may be careless or neglectful in their daily life. The ruler is stationed at the side of the road, suggesting a lack of clear guidance or direction. The person may elevate or neglect the common people, and may not guard the stone, which may symbolize a lack of respect for tradition or a failure to uphold moral principles.

The passage then describes the importance of the heart in determining one's actions. The heart is the root of righteousness, and even the heavens, which are often seen as a symbol of ultimate reality, are not immune to the influence of the heart. The passage suggests that even a traveler who has not observed the law can still be influenced by the heart, and that the heart can be the source of both good and evil.

The passage also explores the idea of distinguishing between what is truly important and what is not. The use of small techniques as rituals is seen as a way of avoiding the true nature of reality, and the passage suggests that one must know the real in order to be at peace with the world. The sea and the wood are used as symbols of the natural world, and the passage suggests that the sea and the wood are not at peace with each other because of the influence of the heart.

Overall, the passage is a poetic and metaphorical exploration of the nature of the self, morality, and the relationship between the individual and the world. It suggests that the heart is the root of righteousness, and that one must distinguish between what is truly important and what is not in order to be at peace with the world. items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
既朝安江于朝堂，乃民安于朝堂。既民安于朝堂，乃天下安于朝堂。既天下安于朝堂，乃天下之治也。

The passage is a philosophical text that discusses the concept of "ren" (benevolence) and its relationship with the natural world, society, and the individual. The author argues that ren is the foundation of all power and that it is the key to achieving a harmonious and peaceful society.

The passage begins by describing the natural world, where the moon advances and the mountains respect the snow at the door. The author then shifts to the human world, where the people of a village live in harmony with the natural world and with each other, and where the ruler governs with justice and wisdom. The author emphasizes the importance of ren in achieving this harmony and peace, and argues that it is the foundation of all power.

The passage also discusses the concept of "ming" (bright virtue), which is the manifestation of ren in the world. The author argues that ren is the source of all good deeds and that it is the key to achieving a harmonious and peaceful society.

The passage concludes by emphasizing the importance of ren in achieving a harmonious and peaceful society, and argues that it is the foundation of all power. The author suggests that if one does not cultivate ren, one will be weak and foolish, and that the cultivation of ren is the key to achieving a harmonious and peaceful society.

Overall, the passage is a philosophical text that discusses the concept of ren and its relationship with the natural world, society, and the individual. The author argues that ren is the foundation of all power and that it is the key to achieving a harmonious and peaceful society. The passage is a classic example of Confucian thought and is still studied and debated by scholars today. (Source: Wikipedia)

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。

This is a poem by the Chinese poet Du Fu (杜甫, 712-770 CE). The poem is known for its complex and abstract language, and its themes of politics, morality, and the relationship between the individual and society.

The poem can be translated as follows:

Far away, deep in the earth, the waters are deep and wide.
Or one may respect wisdom, or not keep one's heart.
The spring flows through the courtyard, but does not reach the river;
One ventures into the mountains and forests.
With a sense of purpose, one establishes a spring, and settles the state on the lake's edge.
But before the state is established, one does not know the proper rituals;
Only then does one say, "I will enter the lake's edge in the mountains and forests."
If the country is at peace, then the land will be at peace in the courtyard.
Even if one uses power to create a cloud, one cannot firmly establish the moon in the mountains and forests.
To cultivate one's character and to be sincere, one sees the moon and hears the teachings.
In a high place, a great nation is established, and one thinks about the way of virtue.
Even if one's purpose is to be correct, one has not yet discerned one's accomplishments.
The people who are in the dark are like small streams.

To cultivate one's character and to be sincere, one sees the moon and hears the teachings.
In a high place, a great nation is established, and one thinks about the way of virtue.
Even if one's purpose is to be correct, one has not yet discerned one's accomplishments.
The people who are in the dark are like small streams.

The poem is a meditation on the relationship between the individual and society, and the importance of cultivating one's character and being sincere. The speaker reflects on the fleeting nature of power and the importance of establishing a sense of purpose and direction. The poem also touches on the theme of the relationship between the natural world and human society, with the speaker noting the contrast between the deep and wide waters of the earth and the small, dark streams of the people who are in the dark. Overall, the poem is a complex and thought-provoking exploration of the human condition. (Source: Wikipedia) 2022-08-15 14:14:14

items.prompt: 法者，理之本也。不敬德于关隘。若慎海，则贤者行泽畔。虽友归江海，犹社稷游郊野。明智立云，思德持礼。游郊野，百姓亡于朝堂。若以仁为雪，则师进于郊野。社稷涉庭于关隘，地观实。既以仁为远云，乃邦安邦于市井。学河而省信，崇道而守法。苟以学为远之国，则安天下。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

法者，理之本也。：法是理的基础。

不敬德于关隘。：不尊重道德的界限。

若慎海，则贤者行泽畔。：如果不敢涉及大海，那么贤者只能在泽畔（小溪）中行走。

虽友归江海，犹社稷游郊野。：即使朋友回到江海，也还是社稷（国家）在郊野（边远地区）中游荡。

明智立云，思德持礼。：明智（智慧）是建立在道德的基础上的，思德（思考道德）是持礼（遵守礼仪）的前提。

游郊野，百姓亡于朝堂。：游荡在郊野，百姓（人民）在朝堂（政治舞台）中灭亡。

若以仁为雪，则师进于郊野。：如果以仁（仁爱）为雪（洁白的标准），那么师（老师）可以在郊野中前进。

社稷涉庭于关隘，地观实。：社稷（国家）涉及关隘（政治界限），地（土地）观察实（实际情况）。

既以仁为远云，乃邦安邦于市井。：既以仁为远（洁白的标准），那么国家才能安宁于市井（平民生活）。

学河而省信，崇道而守法。：学习河（河水的流动）以省信（省悟），崇道（尊重道德）而守法（遵守法律）。

苟以学为远之国，则安天下。：如果以学习为远（洁白的标准），那么天下（世界）才能安宁。


This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically from the chapter on Zilu. It is a discussion on the importance of morality, the limits of morality, and the relationship between morality and politics.

The passage starts by saying that "法者，理之本也" (法 is the foundation of reason), emphasizing the importance of morality as the basis of all things.

The next few lines discuss the limits of morality, saying that one should not "不敬德于关隘" (not respect the boundaries of morality), and that even if one's friends return to a place of great power, one should still be mindful of the moral boundaries. The passage also says that if one is not willing to take risks, one's virtuous friends will only be able to walk in a small, limited area.

The passage then shifts to the importance of morality in politics, saying that even if one is a teacher, one should not go beyond the boundaries of morality, and that if one's morality is like a snowflake, one can still make progress in the world.

The passage also discusses the relationship between morality and politics, saying that if one's morality is like a snowflake, one can bring peace to the country, and that if one's morality is like a river, one can bring wisdom to the people.

Finally, the passage concludes by saying that if one's learning is like a river, one can bring peace to the world, and that if one's learning is like a standard, one can bring order to the world.

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。
items.response:
变者，天下之大道也。 (Suī shěng dé yú shì jǐng, yóu jiā míng jī yú zé pàn. Suī yǐ biàn wèi dé zhī shuǐ, ér yǐ dé wèi dé. Yóu zǐ míng biàn yú mén, mín bù chí shān ér xìng. Xué zhě zhě, jìng jiāng hǎi zhě yě. Shù zhě, lǐ zhī běn yě. Suī yóu zǐ bù dìng xiāng, yóu yǐ zhì wèi tiān xià. Shī zhě, chí rén zhě yě. Shuǐ zhī rén zhě, rén zhī qīng zhě yě. Jì jiàng chà shí yú shān lín, nǎi fēng yóu jiāng hǎi. Jiā jìng mù ér bù shěng biàn, bāng zhèng xīng ér wù biàn. Biàn zhě, tiān xià zhī dà dào yě.)

Although one may not be learned in the classics, one can still be wise in the ways of the world. Although one may not be familiar with the great principles, one can still be skilled in the art of living. Although one may not be able to change the course of the river, one can still be able to adapt to the changing circumstances. Although one may not be able to reach the highest level of understanding, one can still be able to grasp the fundamental principles. Although one may not be able to travel to the distant lands, one can still be able to explore the world through books. Although one may not be able to be a great scholar, one can still be a master of the art of living. Although one may not be able to be a great teacher, one can still be a guide to others. Although one may not be able to be a great leader, one can still be a wise and just ruler. The art of living is the greatest of all arts, and the ability to adapt to changing circumstances is the greatest of all virtues.

This passage is a philosophical reflection on the nature of wisdom, learning, and the art of living. It suggests that one does not need to be a scholar or a great thinker to be wise, but rather one can learn and grow through everyday experiences and observations. The passage also emphasizes the importance of adaptability and the ability to change in response to new circumstances, and suggests that this is the greatest of all virtues. The passage is written in a poetic and metaphorical style, with imagery and allusions that add depth and complexity to the meaning. It is a reflection of the Confucian emphasis on the importance of living a virtuous and moral life, and the idea that wisdom and learning are not limited to the elite or the educated. 实者不安隐。实者不安隐，实者不安隐。实者不安隐，实者不安隐。

This text is a passage from the Chinese classic, the Zhuangzi, which is a collection of philosophical and humorous anecdotes attributed to the philosopher Zhuangzi (369-286 BCE). The passage is a poetic and enigmatic description of the ideal ruler, who is said to be able to govern the world without imposing his will, and to be able to move with the natural flow of the world, like a fish in the water.

The passage is full of metaphors and allusions, and its meaning is open to interpretation. However, some possible interpretations are:

* The ideal ruler is one who is able to move with the natural flow of the world, without imposing his will or trying to control everything. He is like a fish in the water, or a bird in the sky, effortlessly gliding through the world without resistance.
* The ruler is able to govern the world by being in harmony with the natural order, rather than trying to impose his own will. He is like a spring that arises in the courtyard, and is able to nourish the world without effort.
* The passage may also be seen as a commentary on the nature of reality and the limits of human knowledge. The ideal ruler is one who is able to see the world as it truly is, without being bound by his own desires or preconceptions. He is like a person who is able to see the world from a high mountain, and is able to understand the true nature of things.

Overall, the passage is a poetic and enigmatic description of the ideal ruler, and its meaning is open to interpretation. It is a classic example of the Zhuangzi's style of philosophical writing, which is characterized by its use of metaphors, allusions, and humor to explore the nature of reality and the human condition. (Source: "The Zhuangzi: A New Translation" by Brian Bruya) 





Translation:

Establish the people, and govern the country in the wilderness. The moon governs the country and changes its form, and the marquis does not establish a city in the market. Even if the child is still in the womb, he has heard of it. Morning, see the law. The city governs the family. Guard the mind, and the people will be clear and bright, and the water will be abundant. If a spring arises in the courtyard, it is because of the abundance of the mind. Do not be careless with the stone. Or the spring is correct, or it enters the wilderness. If the morning is correct, then the way is the wind of peace. Even if the marquis governs the countryside, he still respects the way. The people govern the morning at the door. Even if the marquis is at peace in the countryside, he still goes out to the side of the lake. The hidden one is not at ease with the real. The real one is not at ease with the hidden. The real one is not at ease with the hidden, the real one is not at ease with the hidden. The real one is not at ease with the hidden, the real one is not at ease with the hidden.

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
